key_insight: Low-symmetry ferromagnetic AFM surfaces can exhibit large, functional spin splitting through surface-induced lifting of sublattice exchange degeneracy, even when net uncompensated magnetization is very small.
engineering_relevance: This suggests a route to spintronic surface design that combines antiferromagnetic robustness and ultrafast dynamics with ferromagnet-like spin splitting.
method: Density functional theory calculations of representative AFM surfaces.
Model Hamiltonians to identify which surface motifs maximize spin splitting.
Compare single-uncompensated-sublattice terminations versus two-sublattice surface truncation cases.
evidence_signal: First-principles results for Cr2O3 and FeF2 show surface spin splittings spanning roughly 10 meV to 1 eV, depending on termination and symmetry.
watch_item: Large splitting depends strongly on surface termination and symmetry, so experimentally realizing the intended low-symmetry ferromagnetic surface may be challenging and materials-specific.
paper_title: Large spin splitting at ferromagnetic surfaces of bulk antiferromagnets
